danger_signals.meaning: Enemy has first-strike advantage; player at tactical disadvantage

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy sprites scattered like NPCs in seamless world
danger_signals.meaning: Danger present but avoidable; player has visual warning and can navigate around threats

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy sprites retreating from player party
danger_signals.meaning: Power differential acknowledged; player is dominant in local area

danger_signals.visual_form: Character sprite state change (implied visual dimming/graying)
danger_signals.meaning: Party member incapacitated; reduced combat effectiveness

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual indicators on character sprites (status condition displays)
danger_signals.meaning: Ongoing damage threat; can trigger game-over if untreated or combined with events

safety_signals.meaning: Player has first-strike advantage; tactical superiority in upcoming battle

safety_signals.visual_form: Battle sequence skipped; enemy defeated instantly on contact
safety_signals.meaning: Encounter is trivially safe; no real threat to party

safety_signals.visual_form: Buildings, NPCs, safe gathering spaces in seamless world
safety_signals.meaning: Non-combat zone; restoration and social interaction available

safety_signals.visual_form: Storefronts and NPC merchants integrated into village scenes
safety_signals.meaning: Resource replenishment available; can restore HP/PP and acquire supplies

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Starman Jr., Starman Deluxe, Final Starman (capsule-contained entities); increasingly complex visual representations
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Eight Sanctuaries scattered across world; player must collect melodies from each to oppose Giygas

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Ness, Paula, Jeff, Poo (distinct character sprites with direction-facing variants)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Party members rendered with jump sprites, sitting sprites, sleeping sprites showing state variations
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Party travels seamlessly through modern Americana setting; Sanctuaries serve as destination waypoints

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Forward-facing static sprite (ghost variant exists in sprite data)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Unused sprite showing dead/knocked-down state (suggests defeated narrative arc)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fourside location; visual clarity on whether enemy survives encounter is ambiguous in final game

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ness begins in pajamas; King dog present during daylight sequences (developer notes indicate night setting added late in development)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Weaker enemies present; automatic victories common; enemies flee from party

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Party expands from Ness to include Paula, Jeff, and Poo; character roster visible in overworld sprite table
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Manual battle sequences required; enemy parties have variable composition; RNG determines item drops and SMAAAASH attacks

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Late game (Moonside, Lost Underworld, endgame areas)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Surreal environments; in-game hallucinations manifest; crates and unusual enemy designs (octopus statues in JP version); tiny NPC sprites appear (photographer, Escargo Express clerk)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy encounters become mandatory; no automatic victories; strategic PSI use required; final boss preparation phase with Ness' Nightmare and Magicant visit

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: SRAM check failure triggers increased encounter rates; Layer Three escalates enemy density visually on overworld
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Layer Four triggers 'Grand Finale' — repeated forced battles with escalating difficulty; gameplay becomes progressively unplayable

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Threed underground prison sequence (mostly deleted, sprite evidence remains)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ness and Paula sitting with eyes closed sprite (unused but in final ROM)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Cutscene drag-to-prison mechanic (removed before release; sprite data preserved)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Honking car emote sprite (unused; intended to show car state via visual indicator)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Environmental congestion visual cue not implemented in final version

environmental_storytelling.technique: Seamless world without overworld map screen
environmental_storytelling.example: No separation between town and outside world; all locations integrated into single massive 10.2 MB map; player navigation communicates unified geography

environmental_storytelling.technique: Visible enemy placement as world-state indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: Enemy sprites scattered like NPCs show local danger level; denser clusters indicate hazardous regions; empty areas signal safety

environmental_storytelling.technique: Oblique projection perspective for narrative immersion
environmental_storytelling.example: 2D isometric-like angle differs from standard RPG grid views; creates intimate, exploratory tone matching Americana setting

environmental_storytelling.technique: Sprite direction-facing variants for character state storytelling
environmental_storytelling.example: Jumping party member sprites convey action moments; King escaping boarding school via direction-faced jump sprite; sleeping Ness with hat shows character routine

environmental_storytelling.technique: Sanctuary locations as visual quest markers
environmental_storytelling.example: Eight geographically distinct Sanctuaries serve as narrative waypoints; melody collection visible through story progression

environmental_storytelling.technique: Unused sprite data as development narrative artifact
environmental_storytelling.example: Deleted Threed prison cutscene sprites (sitting Ness/Paula with closed eyes) visible in ROM; indicate cut content scope without textual documentation
NPC behavior determined by 14,592 sequential RNG values; text scrolling advances RNG; loading enemies shifts RNG; creates illusion of living world responding to player presence

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Auto-flee from Ness and party; automatic victory without battle sequence; grants experience without combat animation
Spawned with RNG-determined makeup; item drops random; SMAAAASH attack frequency RNG-dependent; behavior manipulable via pause-RNG exploit
Rendered unconscious; removed from combat; text notification of critical health triggers game state disruption (can interrupt staircase-locking to enable wall-clipping glitch)
Locked to staircase movement; interrupted by event (health warning or phone call) causes unlock, enabling unintended wall-clipping traversal
Appear in capsule or octahedron form; increasingly complex variants (Jr., Deluxe, Final); directly oppose Sanctuary melody collection objective
Lies on ground in Fourside; unused knocked-down sprite exists, suggesting intended death state not clearly communicated in final version
Rock-lifting animation exists but never displays; intended environmental interaction incomplete
Layer One region check passes; Layer Two SRAM validation fails; Layer Three escalates visual encounter density; Layer Four triggers unwinnable 'Grand Finale' state

notes: EarthBound uses primarily behavioral and composition-based world-state communication rather than explicit visual filters. The seamless world and visible enemy placement create a unified danger landscape where player navigation choices directly interact with threat perception. RNG-driven NPC behavior and enemy composition suggest a world that appears to respond dynamically to player presence. Regional differences (octopus statues in JP vs. pencil erasers in US) indicate cultural localization affecting visual enemy identity without narrative impact. Unused sprite data (Tenda animation, deleted Threed cutscene, Everdred death state) reveals cut narrative beats that would have enriched world-state visual communication. Anti-piracy layers use escalating encounter frequency as punishment mechanic, making visual threat density the primary punitive signal. No explicit faction color-coding or sigil systems documented; faction identity relies on unit types (Starman variants) and location-based sanctuary markers.
